summary: Graham argues that “trolling” has shifted from deliberate provocation to a broader pattern of asshole behavior, often with unclear intent. He identifies four main causes—distance/anonymity, the personality profile common in computer forums, incompetence at articulating disagreement, and permissive forum culture. Left unchecked, trolling follows a Gresham’s Law dynamic that drives out thoughtful participants and becomes dominant.
key_points: The meaning of “troll” expanded from intentional baiting to general abusive behavior.
Anonymity and distance make people say things they would never say face-to-face.
Computer forums attract people more comfortable with ideas than with social tact, amplifying abruptness.
Incompetence at reasoning or explaining disagreement leads to insults as a safer shortcut.
Forum culture sets the ceiling for behavior; if rudeness is tolerated it spreads and crowds out thoughtful users.
money_quotes: There are two senses of the word "troll."
Hackers can be abrupt even in person. Put them on an anonymous forum, and the problem gets worse.
In this respect trolling is a lot like graffiti.
There's a sort of Gresham's Law of trolls: trolls are willing to use a forum with a lot of thoughtful people in it, but thoughtful people aren't willing to use a forum with a lot of trolls in it.
key_insight: Trolling thrives when anonymity, social awkwardness, and low-effort disagreement meet permissive culture, and once it takes hold it drives out thoughtful participants.
